Observatory (GGAO),is shown ranging to the Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter (LRO)

orbiting the Moon. The LRO Laser Ranging (LR) system enables the spacecraft

to achieve its precision orbit determination requirement.

 (GGAO), is the next generation of

 under development.

NASA GSFC's Goddard Geophysical and Astronomical

Observatory (GGAO). The multi-GNSS-capable

receiver will track signals from several GNSS including

GPS, GLONASS, and Galileo.

in the background.  

[Latest Project News](https://space-geodesy.nasa.gov/news/news.html)

The Navy and NASA are jointly preparing an Environmental Impact Statement (EIS) for real estate agreements at PMRF and KPGO

June 20, 2025 

![Image 7: NASA and Navy logos](https://space-geodesy.nasa.gov/news/images/NASA_and_Navy_Logos.jpg) The U.S. Department of the Navy (Navy) and the National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) have jointly prepared a Draft Environmental Impact Statement (EIS) to evaluate the potential environmental impacts of proposed real estate agreements with the State of Hawai‘i for the Pacific Missile Range Facility (PMRF) and the Kōke‘e Park Geophysical Observatory (KPGO). Public involvement is an integral part of the environmental planning process. The public has an important role in providing input during this process to help the Navy and NASA make more informed decisions about the proposed real estate action including on the environmental impacts of the alternatives, environmental or cultural concerns, information the public would like the Navy and NASA to know, and any other information the public would like to see addressed in the Final EIS. 

 Between July 15 and July 17, the Navy and NASA will host public meetings at three locations on Kaua‘i. Each meeting will be held from 5 - 8 PM HST and include a live online broadcast and public comment opportunity. Please visit the PRMF & KPGO EIS website to learn more about the real estate action, the environmental planning process, the public meetings or to register for the live online broadcast, or how to submit comments: [**https://pmrf-kpgo-eis.com/**](https://pmrf-kpgo-eis.com/).

GGOS releases short film highlighting the Terrestrial Reference Frame 

07/29/2024

![Image 8: GGOS logo](https://space-geodesy.nasa.gov/news/images/ggos_logoForNews.jpg) The GGOS (Global Geodetic Observing System) Coordinating Office has now released a new 2.5-minute short film highlighting the critical role of geodetic terrestrial reference frames. This video is primarily aimed at the general public and has been produced in collaboration with the UN-GGCE (United Nations Global Geodetic Center of Excellence). The video is available with subtitles in over 16 languages and can be viewed on the GGOS YouTube channel: [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zfqq-0d2txk](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zfqq-0d2txk).

 This short video complements the more comprehensive 10-minute version ( [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vvNXv05646M](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vvNXv05646M)), available in multiple languages, which has been viewed over 40,000 times since its release in September 2023.

New IGS Analysis Center Coordinator Announced

07/24/2024

![Image 9: IGS logo](https://space-geodesy.nasa.gov/news/images/IGS_logo_forNews.png) The International GNSS Service (IGS) Governing Board and Central Bureau have announced that the next IGS Analysis Center Coordinator (ACC) will be a collaborative effort led by the NASA Goddard Space Flight Center and Geoscience Australia, with support from GFZ German Research Centre for Geosciences Potsdam and the Massachusetts Institute of Technology.

 The ACC is a critical role within the IGS, with the following key responsibilities:

*   Monitoring the quality of products submitted by individual ACs.
*   Combining products from individual ACs to produce robust operational products.
*   Evaluating the combined products for performance and consistency.
*   Disseminating official IGS combined products through an operational framework.
*   Coordinating changes, developments, and improvements within the contributing ACs to use the latest models and standards.
*   Evaluating the suitability of new ACs that wish to be recognized by the IGS.

The ACC is responsible for establishing the detailed specifications for IGS products, while working closely with ACs and the IGS Reference Frame Coordinator. Additionally, the ACC works closely with the ACs in the generation of other IGS analysis products, including the content of the IGS ACC website ([https://igs.org/acc/](https://igs.org/acc)). The new NASA/GA+ ACC will undertake a phased approach to ensure a smooth and thorough transition towards a fully multi-GNSS IGS, enhancing the accuracy and reliability of future GNSS products, while working towards achieving the IGS strategic goal of “multi-GNSS Technical Excellence.”

Lockheed Martin receives Laser Retroflector Arrays for GPS III SV9, SV10

07/10/2024

[![Image 10: Retroreflector Array](https://space-geodesy.nasa.gov/news/images/RetroreflectorArray_100w.png)](https://space-geodesy.nasa.gov/news/images/RetroreflectorArray.png) In partnership with NASA and the National Geospatial-Intelligence Agency, the U.S. Space Force GPS III Program Office has delivered two laser retroflector arrays to Lockheed Martin. On May 6, the LRAs were delivered under a directed program to rapidly integrate hosted payloads on GPS satellites ahead of launch call up. The LRAs will be installed onto two GPS III satellites, SV9 and SV10, in preparation for a 2025 launch.

 The addition of the LRA hosted payload onto GPS satellites marks an expansion of NASA’s Space Geodesy Program space instrument network. The GPS III LRAs will allow NASA to make precise range measurements to the sub-centimeter level using the Satellite Laser Ranging technique, enabling accurate determination of the Earth’s center. The NASA Space Geodesy Program and NGA will use collected SLR data from the GPS III LRAs to maintain a globally available, high quality terrestrial reference frame.

[Read more on spaceforce.mil...](https://www.spaceforce.mil/News/Article-Display/Article/3832649/lockheed-martin-receives-laser-retroflector-arrays-for-gps-iii-sv9-sv10/)

The Navy and NASA are jointly preparing an Environmental Impact Statement (EIS) for real estate agreements at PMRF and KPGO

05/08/2024

![Image 11: NASA and Navy logos](https://space-geodesy.nasa.gov/news/images/NASA_and_Navy_Logos.jpg) The U.S. Navy and the National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) are jointly preparing an Environmental Impact Statement (EIS) to evaluate the potential environmental impacts of proposed real estate agreements with the State of Hawai‘i for the Pacific Missile Range Facility (PMRF) and the Kōkeʻe Park Geophysical Observatory (KPGO). Public involvement is an integral part of the environmental planning process. The public has an important role in providing input during this process to help the Navy and NASA make more informed decisions about the proposed real estate action including potential alternatives, environmental or cultural concerns, information the public would like the Navy and NASA to know, and any other information the public would like to see addressed in the EIS.

 Please visit the PRMF & KPGO EIS website to learn more about the real estate action, the environmental planning process, or how to submit comments: [https://pmrf-kpgo-eis.com/](https://pmrf-kpgo-eis.com/).

Complete Dataset from the Apache Point Lunar Laser Ranging Station Released

04/17/2024

![Image 12: view from the air of Apache Point site.](https://space-geodesy.nasa.gov/news/images/APO_100w.jpg) In February 2024, NASA published a set of normal points from the Apache Point Lunar Laser Ranging Station to the NASA Crustal Dynamics Data Information System ([CDDIS](https://cddis.nasa.gov/)) spanning the entire operations of the station, April 2006 through mid-January 2024. This timespan includes data prior to NASA taking over stewardship of the station that was not previously released in part due to ongoing development of software related to the Absolute Calibration System (ACS) that was installed in late 2016. NASA stewardship of the station helped continue and complete the ACS software development, which prompted a re-processing of all the data to improve its accuracy.

The Navy and NASA are jointly preparing an Environmental Impact Statement (EIS) for state lands at PMRF and KPGO

12/15/2023

![Image 13: view from the air of KPGO site.](https://space-geodesy.nasa.gov/news/images/nskpgo02_site_map_100w.jpg) The Navy and NASA are jointly preparing an Environmental Impact Statement (EIS) to evaluate the potential impacts of real estate agreements with the State of Hawai'i, Department of Land and Natural Resources (DLNR) for state lands at PMRF and KPGO. The Navy and NASA encourage the public to participate in this environmental review process. The Navy and NASA will be initiating a public scoping period and holding public meetings in early 2024. The public can participate during the public scoping period by providing input on the proposed real estate action and issues to be considered in development of the EIS. 

[View fact sheet](https://space-geodesy.nasa.gov/docs/2023/PMRF_EIS_Fact_Sheet_1_Page_Landscape_12_14_23.pdf)

![Image 14](https://space-geodesy.nasa.gov/templates/images/linky_3fa7ffblue.png)[Read all news](https://space-geodesy.nasa.gov/news/news.html)

### Share page

## Advanced Technologies for Navigation and Geodesy | ADVANTAGE

### GFZ Work Packages of Project ADVANTAGE

![](/fileadmin/_processed_/7/9/csm_SchematicConstellation_2ecc44def3.jpeg)

Future generations of European global navigation satellite systems are expected to take advantage of recent breakthroughs in optical clock and optical data communication technology, progressions in atom interferometry and new concepts for autonomous spacecraft operations. Within project ADVANTAGE innovative architectures for future navigation systems are envisaged, designed and developed with wide ranging benefits for society, industry and science.

ADVANTAGE is a joint project of [Deutsches Zentrum für Luft- und Raumfahrt](http://www.dlr.de/dlr/en) (DLR) and GFZ Helmholtz-Zentrum für Geoforschung (GFZ) with support by several external partners. The project is funded for a period of three years by [Hermann von Helmholtz-Gemeinschaft](https://www.helmholtz.de/en/)(HGF) within the framework of the "Impuls- und Vernetzungsfond" (Initiative and Networking Fund).

ADVANTAGE represents a joint effort of physicists, geodesists and engineers at the Helmholtz institutions DLR and GFZ. The project contributes to [programme-oriented funding programs](https://www.helmholtz.de/en/research/program_oriented_funding/) (POF) "Space", "Geosystem: The Changing Earth" and "Atmosphere and Climate". ADVANTAGE utilizes synergistic effects between important elements of these POF activities and thus further strengthens the impact of [HGF](https://www.helmholtz.de/en/).

**Coordinator:** Prof. H. Schuh

**Personnel:** K. Balidakis, G. Beyerle, G. Dick, Prof. F. Flechtner, H. Ge, M. Ge, S. Glaser, C. Falck, R. Heinkelmann, R. König, F. H. Massmann, G. Michalak, M. Murböck, K. H. Neumayer, T. Nilsson, M. Semmling, Prof. J. Wickert, F. Zus.

**Time frame:** March 2017 to December 2020

**Reference:**   
Advanced Technologies for Satellite Navigation and Geodesy. G. Giorgi, T.D. Schmidt, C. Trainotti, R. Mata-Calvo, C. Fuchs, M.M. Hoque, J. Berdermann, J. Furthner, C. Günther, T. Schuldt, J. Sanjuan, M. Gohlke, M. Oswald, C. Braxmaier, K. Balidakis, G. Dick, F. Flechtner, M. Ge, S. Glaser, R. König, G. Michalak, M. Murböck, M. Semmling, H. Schuh. Advances in Space Research, 2019, 64(6), pp. 1256-1273. [DOI: 10.1016/j.asr.2019.06.01](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0273117719304260)

**File download**

A summary of the main project objectives is available at this link: [**ADVANTAGE White Paper** (pdf file, 41 KB)](/fileadmin/gfz/sec11/image/images-projects/ADVANTAGE/DraftWhitePaperADVANTAGE-180213.pdf "Initiates file download")

SUMMARY:
Other technological advancements include the use of Very Long Baseline Interferometry (VLBI), Satellite Laser Ranging (SLR), and

FULL CONTENT:
![Project Geospatial](//images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/5eca9471dfb4ed54d3f21688/1590334771804-EC0L0BKGC1IQR5QYIIAX/Project+Geo+Banner.png?format=1500w)
![Project Geospatial](//images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/5eca9471dfb4ed54d3f21688/1590334771804-EC0L0BKGC1IQR5QYIIAX/Project+Geo+Banner.png?format=1500w)

# The Geodesy Crisis in the United States: A Comprehensive Analysis of its Importance, Workforce Challenges, and Potential Consequences

![](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/5eca9471dfb4ed54d3f21688/627d1716-57e5-4013-87c3-1ab7b8d6b5f2/Geodesy+Crisis.png)

#### **The Emerging Geodesy Crisis in the United States**

Geodesy, the science dedicated to precisely measuring and understanding the Earth's geometric shape, its orientation in space, and its gravity field, forms a foundational pillar for numerous critical aspects of modern society. From the creation of accurate maps and navigation systems to the monitoring of environmental changes and the development of infrastructure, geodesy underpins a vast array of applications that are often taken for granted. The United States has a rich history in geodetic surveying, dating back to the early 19th century, and has been at the forefront of advancements in this field. However, a growing concern within the scientific and engineering communities is the emerging crisis in the geodesy workforce, threatening the nation's capacity to maintain and advance this essential science. With this write-up we aim to provide a comprehensive analysis of this crisis, detailing the definition, applications, and history of geodesy in the US, highlighting the organizations that rely on it, describing the necessary tradecraft and education, examining the workforce challenges, and exploring the potential consequences if this crisis remains unaddressed. Understanding the multifaceted nature of this issue is crucial for developing effective strategies to ensure the continued availability of geodetic expertise for the benefit of the nation. Additionally, knowing the many funding dynamics and adjacent markets is crucial for addressing the workforce crisis and ensuring the continued advancement of geodesy, which ultimately benefits numerous sectors of the US economy.

#### **Defining Geodesy: The Science of Earth Measurement**

![](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/5eca9471dfb4ed54d3f21688/fc9c890b-e18e-4450-afe3-d9a89d0c80f2/image_fx_+%2812%29.jpg)

At its core, geodesy is the scientific discipline concerned with the precise measurement and understanding of three fundamental properties of the Earth: its geometric shape, its orientation in space, and its gravity field. This involves assigning precise coordinates to points across the Earth's surface, essentially providing a unique "address" for any location. Historically, these coordinates were determined through terrestrial surveying methods, involving the measurement of distances and angles between points. Today, while traditional techniques still hold relevance, geodesists heavily rely on space-based technologies such as the Global Positioning System (GPS) and other Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS) to achieve unprecedented levels of accuracy in their measurements.

The shape of the Earth is a complex concept in geodesy. While a simple mathematical model like an ellipsoid captures the Earth's general flattened sphere shape due to its rotation, a more detailed representation is provided by the geoid. The geoid approximates the mean sea level across the globe and accounts for the variations in the Earth's gravity field, offering a more accurate depiction of the planet's actual form. Furthermore, geodesy is not a static science; it also involves monitoring how these fundamental properties of the Earth change over time. This includes tracking crustal motion due to plate tectonics, measuring sea-level rise as a consequence of climate change, and observing variations in the Earth's rotation. The ability to make these extremely precise measurements, often down to the centimeter level, allows scientists to study a wide range of dynamic processes affecting our planet.

#### **The Broad Spectrum of Applications of Geodesy in the US**

The applications of geodesy are diverse and permeate numerous sectors within the United States, providing essential support for activities ranging from everyday navigation to advanced scientific research.

**Mapping and Surveying:** Geodesy forms the fundamental basis for all accurate mapping and surveying activities in the US. Organizations like the National Geodetic Survey (NGS) use geodetic principles to define and maintain the National Spatial Reference System (NSRS), a consistent coordinate system that ensures the compatibility of different maps and geospatial datasets. This framework is crucial for defining land boundaries, mapping the US shoreline, and improving transportation and navigation safety. Various surveying techniques, including triangulation, trilateration, leveling, and the use of GNSS, are employed in geodetic surveying to establish precise control points and measure distances and elevations over large areas. These surveys are essential for creating topographic maps, cadastral surveys for property boundaries, engineering surveys for construction projects, and hydrographic surveys for mapping underwater features. *This is crucial for efficient commerce, public safety, and the effective management of land resources.*

**Construction:** The construction industry in the US heavily relies on geodesy for a wide range of applications, from initial site surveying to the precise placement of structures. Geodetic surveys are used to create accurate maps and plans for construction projects, including roads, bridges, dams, airports, and ports. GNSS technology plays a significant role in modern construction, enabling efficient site organization, equipment tracking, precise grading and excavation, and automated machine control. Geodetic surveying also ensures the accurate alignment and stability of large infrastructure projects, detects settlement in structures, and monitors deformations during and after construction. The precision offered by geodetic techniques contributes to increased efficiency, reduced costs, and enhanced safety in the construction sector. *Geodesy's accuracy prevents costly failures, ensures structural integrity, and safeguards public well-being in construction projects.*

**Environmental Monitoring:** Geodesy provides critical tools for monitoring various environmental phenomena in the United States. Satellite geodesy techniques, such as GPS, Gravity Recovery and Climate Experiment (GRACE), and Interferometric Synthetic Aperture Radar (InSAR), are used to study the Earth's geodynamics, including crustal deformation, tectonic plate movements, and the impact of droughts. Geodetic measurements are essential for tracking glacial melt and sea-level rise, providing crucial data for climate change research. GNSS can also serve as a hydrometeorological sensor for monitoring atmospheric water vapor, soil moisture, and terrestrial water storage variations. Furthermore, geodesy plays a role in assessing the impact of disasters on natural resources and in monitoring ground deformation caused by activities like CO2 injection for enhanced oil recovery. *This data is vital for understanding our changing planet and developing strategies for environmental sustainability and resilience.*

**Navigation Systems:** Accurate geodetic frameworks are fundamental to the functioning of navigation systems in the US, including GPS and other GNSS. Geodesy provides the precise positioning data and the consistent coordinate reference systems necessary for these systems to operate reliably. The NSRS, maintained by the NGS, ensures that positioning information is accurate and consistent across the nation, supporting transportation on land, sea, and in the air. This is crucial for aviation safety, maritime navigation, and the efficient operation of intelligent transport systems. The development of precise point positioning techniques based on satellite geodesy has revolutionized navigation, allowing for highly accurate location determination anytime and anywhere. *Reliable navigation is essential for the transportation and logistics sectors, as well as for emergency response and daily activities.*

**Agriculture:** The agriculture sector in the US increasingly utilizes geodetic systems for precision farming practices. GPS and GNSS technologies enable farmers to precisely map their fields, optimize planting patterns, monitor crop growth, and implement targeted interventions such as variable rate application of fertilizers and pesticides. Geodetic systems also assist in soil mapping and analysis, precision irrigation, and yield mapping, leading to increased crop production, efficient resource management, and environmentally sustainable farming practices. The ability to work during low visibility conditions and accurately navigate fields enhances productivity and reduces costs in agriculture. *Geodesy contributes to food security, resource conservation, and economic viability in the agricultural sector.*

**Disaster Management:** Geospatial information, underpinned by geodesy, is critical for all phases of disaster management in the United States, including prediction, preparedness, response, and recovery. GIS systems, which rely on accurate geodetic data, are used to identify areas at risk for natural disasters, predict their impact, and plan evacuation routes and resource allocation. Following a disaster, the NGS conducts aerial surveys to assess damages, providing georeferenced imagery that is crucial for emergency managers, search and rescue efforts, and damage assessment. Geodetic techniques like GPS can also monitor ground deformations that may precede earthquakes or volcanic eruptions, contributing to early warning systems. *Geodesy's contribution to disaster management saves lives, protects property, and facilitates efficient recovery efforts.*

Another critical aspect of disaster management where geodesy plays a crucial role is in **bathymetry**, the measurement of water depth. Geodetic principles are essential for creating accurate bathymetric maps, which are used for:

**Coastal hazard assessment:** Understanding the underwater topography helps in predicting and mitigating the impact of storm surges, tsunamis, and coastal erosion.

**Navigation safety:** Accurate bathymetry is vital for safe navigation of ships, especially in shallow or complex coastal areas.

**Search and rescue operations:** Bathymetric data can aid in planning and conducting underwater search and rescue missions. The precise measurements of the Earth's shape and gravity field, a core component of geodesy, directly influence the accuracy of bathymetric mapping and its effectiveness in disaster management and mitigation.

**Geodesy's Critical Role in Transportation and Logistics:** Geodesy plays an absolutely vital role in the transportation and logistics sectors, forming the backbone for efficient movement of goods and people across the nation. The precision offered by geodetic science is not merely beneficial but essential for a multitude of operations:

Precise Navigation: Geodesy underpins the accuracy of GPS and GNSS, which are fundamental to navigation systems used in vehicles, ships, aircraft, and even pedestrian navigation. This ensures accurate routing, reduces errors, and enhances safety across all modes of transportation.

Infrastructure Development: The construction of transportation infrastructure, including roads, railways, bridges, ports, and airports, relies heavily on geodetic surveying for accurate planning, design, and construction. Geodetic data ensures proper alignment, grading, and stability of these structures, preventing costly errors and ensuring long-term safety.

Logistics Optimization: Geodesy contributes to the efficient management of supply chains and logistics operations. Accurate location data and mapping enable precise tracking of goods, optimization of delivery routes, and improved coordination of transportation networks. This leads to reduced transportation costs, timely deliveries, and enhanced overall efficiency in the movement of goods.

Autonomous Transportation: As the development of autonomous vehicles accelerates, geodesy becomes even more critical. Self-driving cars, trucks, and ships rely on highly accurate and reliable positioning data, provided by geodetic systems, to navigate safely and efficiently.

In essence, geodesy is the unseen force that guides and shapes the way we move, connect, and transport goods, making it indispensable to the functioning of the entire transportation and logistics ecosystem.

#### **A Journey Through Time: The History of Geodesy in the United States**

The history of geodesy in the United States is a rich and dynamic narrative, deeply intertwined with the nation's territorial expansion, scientific progress, and technological evolution. From its nascent beginnings in the early 19th century, driven by the need for accurate coastal charting and maritime safety, geodesy in the U.S. has undergone a transformative journey. This section will explore the pivotal milestones, key figures, and technological advancements that have shaped the development of geodesy in the United States, tracing its evolution from traditional terrestrial surveying methods to the era of sophisticated space-based technologies. Understanding this historical context is crucial for appreciating the present state of geodesy and the challenges it faces, as well as for informing strategies to ensure its continued advancement and contributions to the nation.

**Early Beginnings (1807-1878):** The foundation of US geodesy was laid with the establishment of the Survey of the Coast in 1807 by President Thomas Jefferson. This marked the nation's first civilian scientific agency, tasked with surveying the coastline to create nautical charts and enhance maritime safety. Ferdinand R. Hassler, a Swiss geodesist, was appointed as the first superintendent and played a pivotal role in setting high standards for accuracy in American geodetic work. The initial work involved triangulation surveys near New York City, establishing a framework of precisely measured points. As the nation expanded westward, the survey efforts extended inland. In 1836, the agency was renamed the United States Coast Survey, and in 1878, reflecting the increasing importance of geodesy, it became the United States Coast and Geodetic Survey (USC&GS).

**Laying the Foundations (1878-1940):** This period saw significant advancements in geodetic surveying and the establishment of national networks. The USC&GS focused on building a national geodetic control network to ensure the consistency of maps and charts across the country. Key milestones included the completion of the Eastern Oblique Arc of Triangulation in 1889 and the Transcontinental Arc in 1896. Geodetic leveling also progressed, with the completion of a transcontinental line of spirit levels in 1907. The first general adjustment of the geodetic leveling network in the US was made in 1900, incorporating data from various surveying agencies. The North American Datum of 1927 (NAD27) and the National Geodetic Vertical Datum of 1929 (NGVD29) were significant achievements of this era, providing horizontal and vertical reference systems for the nation. Technological advancements during this time included the adoption of invar tapes for more accurate baseline measurements and the development of the Bilby steel tower for triangulation.

**The Modern Era (1940-Present):** The latter half of the 20th century and the beginning of the 21st century witnessed a revolution in geodesy with the advent of space-based technologies. In 1970, the USC&GS was abolished, and its geodetic responsibilities were placed under the newly formed National Geodetic Survey (NGS) within the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA). Satellite geodesy emerged as a powerful tool, with the first use of artificial satellites for geodetic positioning occurring in the 1960s. The development and deployment of the NAVSTAR Global Positioning System (GPS) marked a transformative shift, allowing for highly precise measurements of the Earth's surface. The NGS played a crucial role in the early research and development of GPS applications in geodesy. Other technological advancements include the use of Very Long Baseline Interferometry (VLBI), Satellite Laser Ranging (SLR), and Interferometric Synthetic Aperture Radar (InSAR) for studying geodynamic phenomena. The NGS continues to modernize the national geodetic datums, with the planned release of the North American Terrestrial Reference Frame of 2022 (NATRF2022) and the North American-Pacific Geopotential Datum of 2022 (NAPGD2022) to replace NAD83 and NAVD88, respectively.

#### **The Indispensable Role of Geodesy: Why It Matters**

Geodesy plays a role that is nothing short of critical in the functioning of modern society within the United States. It provides the fundamental spatial framework that underpins a vast and diverse range of essential activities upon which the nation depends. From enabling the precise construction of infrastructure and ensuring the reliable operation of navigation systems to supporting crucial land management practices and facilitating cutting-edge scientific research, the contributions of geodesy are both foundational and far-reaching. This section will elucidate the indispensable role of geodesy in these key areas, demonstrating why a robust and thriving geodetic capability is essential for the continued progress, safety, and economic prosperity of the United States.

**Infrastructure Development:** Accurate geodetic control is indispensable for the planning, design, and construction of all types of infrastructure, including transportation networks (roads, railways, airports, ports), utilities (pipelines, power lines), and buildings (skyscrapers, dams). Without precise geodetic measurements, engineers would lack the necessary spatial information to ensure the accurate alignment, stability, and safety of these critical structures. Errors in geodetic data can lead to costly mistakes, delays, and even catastrophic failures in infrastructure projects.

**Navigation Systems:** As previously mentioned, geodesy is the bedrock of all modern navigation systems, most notably GPS. These systems are integral to countless aspects of daily life, from personal navigation using smartphones to the guidance of aircraft and ships. The accuracy and reliability of these navigation systems, which are essential for transportation, logistics, and emergency response, depend entirely on the precise geodetic reference frames and positioning data provided by geodesists.

**Land Management:** Geodesy is fundamental to the accurate definition and management of land boundaries and property rights. Cadastral surveys, based on geodetic principles, establish legal property lines, which are crucial for land ownership, taxation, and resolving boundary disputes. Accurate geodetic data also supports Geographic Information Systems (GIS) used by local governments and property owners for land-use planning, zoning, and infrastructure management.

**Scientific Research:** Geodesy provides essential data for a wide range of scientific disciplines. Geodetic measurements are used to study plate tectonics, monitor crustal deformation, and understand the dynamics of the Earth's interior. The precise tracking of satellite orbits, which is crucial for Earth observation missions studying climate change, sea-level rise, and ice mass loss, relies heavily on geodesy. Furthermore, variations in the Earth's gravity field, measured by geodetic techniques, provide insights into the distribution of mass within the planet.

**Economic Benefits:** The applications of geodesy generate significant economic benefits for the United States. Accurate geodetic control reduces costs and improves efficiency in various sectors, including construction, transportation, and agriculture. Studies have estimated that the NSRS alone provides billions of dollars in potential annual benefits to the US economy. Improved floodplain management, enabled by precise geodetic heights, can lead to substantial savings. The ability to collect, manage, and align geospatial information accurately, which is underpinned by geodesy, is essential for societal, environmental, and economic activities. The economic benefits of geodesy extend into several adjacent markets. These include:

**Geospatial Technology:** The development and manufacturing of GNSS receivers, surveying instruments, GIS software, and other geospatial technologies represent a significant market that heavily relies on geodesy.

**Construction and Engineering:** The large-scale construction and civil engineering industries are major consumers of geodetic services, driving demand for surveyors and geodesists.

**Agriculture:** Precision agriculture, enabled by geodetic systems, is a growing market with increasing reliance on accurate positioning and mapping.

**Funding for geodesy comes from a variety of sources:**

**Government Agencies:** Federal agencies like the NGS, USGS, NASA, and FEMA allocate funds for geodetic research, infrastructure maintenance, and data collection.

**Research Grants:** Universities and research institutions often secure funding through grants from government agencies and private foundations to support geodetic research and development.

**Private Sector Investment:** Private surveying and engineering firms, as well as geospatial technology companies, invest in geodetic technologies and services.

#### **Key Organizations at the Forefront of Geodesy in the US**

Several key government agencies and private sector organizations in the United States heavily rely on and employ geodesists, playing crucial roles in advancing and applying this science.

| Organization | Sector | Reliance on Geodesists | Primary Role |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| National Geodetic Survey (NGS) | Government (NOAA) | Extremely High | Defines and manages the National Spatial Reference System (NSRS); conducts geodetic research and surveys; provides geodetic data and tools. |
| United States Geological Survey (USGS) | Government | High | Conducts scientific research on Earth's processes, including crustal deformation and seismic activity; produces maps and geospatial data. |
| Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) | Government | Moderate to High | Relies on accurate height data for floodplain mapping and disaster response efforts. |
| National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) | Government | High | Conducts space geodesy research; uses geodetic techniques for satellite tracking, Earth observation, and understanding Earth's shape and gravity field. |
| Bureau of Land Management (BLM) | Government | Moderate | Responsible for surveying and managing federal lands; requires geodesists for boundary determination and land management. |
| United States Army Corps of Engineers (USACE) | Government | Moderate | Involved in civil engineering projects, including surveying and mapping for infrastructure development and flood control. |
| Private Surveying and Engineering Firms | Private Sector | Extremely High | Employ licensed surveyors and geodesists to conduct land surveys, construction staking, and provide geospatial services to various clients. |
| Geospatial Technology Companies | Private Sector | Moderate to High | Develop and provide GNSS receivers, surveying instruments, GIS software, and other technologies used in geodesy and related fields; often employ geodesists for research and development. |
| Universities and Research Institutions | Academic/Research | Moderate | Conduct research in geodesy, geophysics, and related fields; educate the next generation of geodesists. |

The **National Geodetic Survey (NGS)**, a part of NOAA, stands as the primary federal agency responsible for defining, maintaining, and providing access to the NSRS. The NGS employs a significant number of geodesists who are involved in geodetic research, data collection, and the development of geodetic models and tools. Their work underpins a vast range of applications across the nation.

The **United States Geological Survey (USGS)** also employs geodesists in its mission to study the Earth, focusing on areas such as earthquake hazards, volcanic activity, and crustal deformation. Geodetic techniques, particularly GPS and InSAR, are crucial for monitoring these phenomena.

**FEMA** relies on accurate geodetic height data for its floodplain mapping program, which is essential for insurance purposes and for guiding development in flood-prone areas. Geodesists contribute to the development of these maps and models.

**NASA** plays a vital role in advancing space geodesy, utilizing techniques like VLBI and SLR to precisely track satellites, study the Earth's rotation, and monitor changes in the planet's shape and gravity field. Their Space Geodesy Project aims to develop and deploy the next generation of geodetic stations.

In the **private sector**, numerous surveying and engineering firms employ licensed professional surveyors, many of whom possess expertise in geodesy. These professionals provide essential geospatial services to a wide range of clients, including land developers, construction companies, and government agencies. Additionally, companies that develop geospatial technologies often employ geodesists for research, development, and technical support.

**Universities and research institutions** across the US also employ geodesists who conduct fundamental research, develop new methodologies, and educate students in this critical field. These institutions are vital for ensuring a future pipeline of qualified geodesists.

However, it's important to note that the number of universities in the US offering dedicated geodesy programs has been declining in recent decades, leading to a decrease in graduates with specialized expertise. This trend puts the US at a disadvantage compared to other nations that maintain robust geodesy education programs, threatening to erode the country's leadership in this critical field.

#### **The Geodesist's Toolkit: Tradecraft, Education, and Qualifications**

The practice of modern geodesy demands a unique and complex combination of skills, techniques, and technologies, necessitating specialized education and rigorous qualifications for professionals entering and advancing within the field. Here we will highlight the essential tradecraft of a geodesist, encompassing the diverse skills, techniques, and cutting-edge technologies employed to measure and understand our dynamic Earth. It will also outline the typical educational pathways and the qualifications that aspiring geodesists must pursue to build a successful career in this critical science.

**Tradecraft (Skills, Techniques, and Technologies):** Modern geodesists utilize a wide array of techniques and technologies to make precise measurements of the Earth. These include:

**Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS):** Utilizing signals from satellite constellations like GPS, GLONASS, Galileo, and BeiDou to determine precise positions on the Earth's surface. This involves expertise in operating GNSS receivers, processing GNSS data using specialized software, and understanding potential error sources.

**Terrestrial Surveying:** Employing traditional instruments like total stations, levels, and theodolites to measure angles and distances on the ground. This requires skills in setting up and operating the equipment, performing accurate measurements, and applying trigonometric principles for calculations.

**Space Geodesy Techniques:** Utilizing advanced methods such as Very Long Baseline Interferometry (VLBI), which measures radio waves from distant quasars; Satellite Laser Ranging (SLR), which measures the distance to satellites using laser pulses; and Doppler Orbitography and Radiopositioning Integrated by Satellite (DORIS), which tracks satellite orbits using ground beacons. These techniques require specialized knowledge of radio astronomy, laser technology, and satellite orbital mechanics.

**Remote Sensing:** Analyzing aerial photographs and satellite imagery using photogrammetry and other remote sensing techniques to create maps and models of the Earth's surface. This involves skills in image processing, geometric correction, and feature extraction.

**Gravimetric Surveying:** Measuring variations in the Earth's gravitational field using gravity meters to study the Earth's density and mass distribution.

**Data Analysis and Modeling:** Processing and analyzing large datasets collected from various geodetic techniques using specialized software and programming languages like Python and MATLAB. This includes applying statistical methods, developing mathematical models of the Earth, and interpreting the results.

**Geographic Information Systems (GIS):** Utilizing GIS software to manage, analyze, and visualize geospatial data, including geodetic measurements, for various applications.

**Educational Pathways and Qualifications:** Becoming a professional geodesist in the US typically requires a strong educational foundation in a related field. Common educational pathways include:

**Bachelor's Degree:** A bachelor's degree in geodesy, geomatics, surveying engineering, geophysics, mathematics, physics, or a related Earth science is generally the minimum requirement for entry-level positions. These programs typically include coursework in geodetic principles, mathematical modeling, data analysis, surveying techniques, and geospatial technologies.

**Master's Degree:** For more advanced research positions, management roles, and government jobs, a master's degree in geodesy, geop

# NASA’s Space Geodesy Project Mapping Out a Bright Future

![The headshot image of NASA](https://www.nasa.gov/wp-content/uploads/2023/04/cropped-nasa-logo-wallpaper-300x300.jpeg)

### NASA

In April 2019, an international team of more than 300 scientists unveiled the first recorded images of a black hole, its dark shadow and vivid orange disk peering back across 55 million light years of space. Capturing images from so far away required the combined power of eight radio telescopes across four continents, working together to essentially form a massive Earth-sized telescope called the [Event Horizon Telescope](https://eventhorizontelescope.org/) (EHT).

The technology that powers EHT imaging is also used by scientists at NASA and worldwide to measure the Earth. Very long baseline interferometry, or VLBI, is a technique that combines waveforms recorded by two or more radio telescopes. This versatile tool is used not only in astronomy, but also geodesy: The science of measuring Earth’s size, shape, rotation and orientation in space.

Geodesy lets us see maps on our phones, measure ocean tides, plan rocket launches, calibrate clocks, forecast earthquakes, track tsunamis and maintain satellite orbits. As a geodetic tool, VLBI helps scientists precisely measure distances and topography and track changes to Earth’s surface and rotation over time. Scientists at NASA’s [Goddard Space Flight Center](https://www.nasa.gov/goddard) in Greenbelt, Maryland, and MIT’s [Haystack Observatory](https://www.haystack.mit.edu/) in Westford, Massachusetts, pioneered the geodetic use of VLBI in the 1960s.

Today, NASA, MIT Haystack and other partners collaborate to improve and expand geodetic stations around the world as part of NASA’s [Space Geodesy Project](https://space-geodesy.nasa.gov/index.html) (SGP). MIT Haystack serves as the hub for hardware and software development that benefits both astronomy and geodesy, partnering with the [National Science Foundation](https://www.nsf.gov/) to support the EHT and with NASA to boost the SGP. Together, that synergy contributed directly to realizing the black hole image while working towards smaller, faster radio telescopes, more automation and access to multiple geodetic tools in the same place, enabling more precise maps, charts, flight paths and orbits than ever before.

**VLBI: All About That Baseline**

Radio telescopes measure radio waves. These waves are weaker and fainter than visible light, but they penetrate interstellar dust and gases and interference from Earth’s own atmosphere in ways that visible light cannot. They also give astronomers information about space that does not exist in the visible spectrum.

![A satellite dish pointed up at the sky.](https://www.nasa.gov/wp-content/uploads/2019/10/img_6847.jpg?w=1571)

Viewing radio waves requires large, sensitive telescopes. A VLBI array has magnifying power, or “angular resolution,” equivalent to a single telescope with a dish as wide as the longest baseline between two telescopes in the array. (For example, the EHT’s most distant telescopes were separated by more than 7,000 miles, equaling a single telescope more than twice as wide as the United States.) Each telescope in the EHT array captured the radio waves emitted by the black hole from a unique angle, depending on their location on Earth. Adding all these observations together with a powerful computer yielded the final images.

In the 1960’s, scientists at NASA Goddard and MIT Haystack realized that this slight difference in perspective was a valuable source of information — not just about space, but about Earth.

“The basic principle of geodetic VLBI is that radio waves coming in from a distant source hit one station before the other,” said [Stephen Merkowitz](https://space-geodesy.nasa.gov/multimedia/staff_profiles/Stephen_Merkowitz/MerkowitzVideoProfile.html), manager of NASA’s [Space Geodesy Project](https://space-geodesy.nasa.gov/about/projOverview.html). “We use quasars, which are very distant active galaxies, so far away that they are fixed points in the sky. We measure the time delay between when the signal hits those two points, and convert to a distance using the speed of light.”

Earth’s rotation causes changes to the time delay between the quasar signals observed by the VLBI stations, allowing scientists to precisely measure the rotation’s speed. They can also use this data to measure the location and distance between the VLBI stations, and by repeating these measurements over time, can observe even tiny, slow changes to the Earth’s surface, like continental drift.

Perhaps VLBI’s most important function is helping to build the International Terrestrial and Celestial Reference Frames. The Terrestrial Reference Frame assigns coordinates to locations on Earth, including its center, providing a consistent framework to relate measurements to each other.

“Suppose you have a mission that measures the sea level in the Gulf of Mexico and have a tide gauge off the coast of Louisiana that is also taking sea level measurements, and you want to tie those together so you have some ground truth to the space observations,” Merkowitz said. “If they’re not in the same reference frame, you can’t do that. If your frame isn’t precise and stable, that will introduce all kinds of errors into that tie. So, a good reference frame allows you to connect different data sets through geolocation.”

The Celestial Reference Frame serves a similar purpose, but instead of creating a stable framework for Earth locations, it creates a framework for locating astronomical objects. Scientists use Earth Orientation Parameters — measurements of time, orientation and rotation — to link the two frames together. This creates a total system to geolocate objects in space and on Earth.

One example of an everyday technology that depends on these reference frames is the [Global Positioning System](https://www.gps.gov/), or GPS. GPS relies on a constellation of satellites constantly broadcasting their locations and times to GPS-enabled devices on the ground, from cell phones to farm equipment. The satellites in the constellation rely on the Terrestrial Reference Frame and the Earth Orientation Parameters to relay their location, so keeping those frameworks precise and accurate is essential for daily activities around the world.

Quantities we sometimes take for granted, like the length of Earth’s day and how fast it rotates, are not actually constant, Merkowitz said. “They depend on lots of different things like weather, major mass motions like El Niño or La Niña, and movements of large amounts of water,” he explained.

**Charting a Course for Geodesy’s Future**

With Earth constantly changing, geodesy keeps maps accurate, planes and ships on course and satellite measurements precise. In fact, VLBI and other tools are vital for Earth-observing satellites like [ICESat-2](https://icesat-2.gsfc.nasa.gov/) and instruments like [GEDI](https://gedi.umd.edu), both of which use laser pulses to measure the structures of ice sheets and forests. Without knowing exactly where the spacecraft are located above Earth’s surface, scientists wouldn’t be able to make these kind of precision measurements.

“When you do precision orbit determination for something like ICESat-2, it requires the reference frame as input,” Merkowitz said. “ICESat-2 is very sensitive to errors, so if the calculation of Earth’s center is off, it translates into an error in the science measurements. Precision orbit missions and missions that measure heights are particularly dependent on the framework.”

In 2007, the [National Academy of Sciences](http://nasonline.org) reported that the nation’s geodesy infrastructure was aging too quickly to keep up with growing demands for data. So NASA launched the Space Geodesy Project to develop and deploy the next generation of geodetic stations, which includes VLBI as well as other techniques that use lasers to precisely track satellites (called satellite laser ranging, or SLR).

The new VLBI stations will be able to sample across a broad range of frequencies instead of just two, giving them more flexibility to keep collecting data if there is interference from Wi-Fi or other signals. Their smaller size and quicker movement will make them more adaptable to atmospheric conditions, but to make up for the smaller dishes (remember, with radio telescopes, bigger is better), they will sample data much faster. Eventually, Merkowitz said, the systems will be able to collect data 24 hours a day without human supervision to provide much more rapid measurements.

The National Academy of Sciences and other international geodetic associations recommend that, for the best science outcomes, the updated Space Geodesy Network should be accurate to within one millimeter, or about the thickness of an ID card. It should also be stable to within one-tenth of a millimeter — the width of a human hair. This precision is crucial for measuring sea levels, which are [increasing](https://climate.nasa.gov/news/2680/new-study-finds-sea-level-rise-accelerating/) at about 3.4 millimeters, or 0.13 inches, per year, Merkowitz said.

The project is in its first phase: replacing NASA’s domestic geodetic stations with the next-generation systems.  NASA recently installed its third domestic VLBI station in Texas; its next-generation VLBI stations in Hawaii and Maryland are already in operation and routinely taking measurements.

NASA is also working with international partners to help transition the international VLBI network to the next-generation technology, Merkowitz said. “International cooperation is vital to the success of space geodesy. Measuring global effects requires a global network, and NASA cannot do this alone.”

The next-generation network will support more precise GPS, increasingly precise reference frames and better support for the many ways we use maps in today’s world. With the help of VLBI, the team will help us know where we are – and where we’re going – with even more accuracy in the future.

By: Jessica Merzdorf  
[NASA’s Goddard Space Flight Center, Greenbelt](https://www.nasa.gov/goddard), Md.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Integrating optical technologies and advancements like VLBI and SLR enhances precision in satellite navigation, improving accuracy in Earth measurements crucial for space exploration.

Joint efforts between geodesists, engineers, and scientists foster innovation, enabling comprehensive solutions for navigation challenges, ultimately enhancing the functionality of systems like GPS and space missions.
